name: David Soria Parra
bio_arc: 20+ years in developer tooling and static analysis. ~10 years at Facebook/Meta (static analysis, developer tooling, acquisition integration including Oculus), then returned to individual contributor work. Co-created the Model Context Protocol (MCP) alongside Justin Spahr-Summers at Anthropic. Now Lead Maintainer of the MCP specification and Member of Technical Staff at Anthropic. Keynote at Linux Foundation's MCP Dev Summit NA (April 2026). Moved MCP governance to Agentic AI Foundation under Linux Foundation. Arc from developer tooling expertise through social-scale engineering to defining the standard protocol for AI assistant-tool integration.

canonical_works.title: Model Context Protocol Specification
canonical_works.why_it_matters: The canonical protocol definition for how AI assistants discover, invoke, and manage external tools and data sources -- directly governs the MCP server interface that cross-validated-search exposes.

canonical_works.title: MCP Server SDK (Python/TypeScript)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Reference implementation patterns for building MCP servers including stdio transport, tool registration, schema definition, and error handling -- the exact patterns used by cross-validated-search-mcp.

canonical_works.title: MCP Dev Summit NA 2026 Keynote
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Presented the roadmap for MCP including elicitation, tasks, MCP apps, streaming HTTP transport, and discoverability -- forward guidance for evolving the MCP integration surface.

canonical_works.title: a16z Podcast: MCP Co-Creator on the Next Wave of LLM Innovation
canonical_works.why_it_matters: In-depth interview revealing design philosophy: pain-point-first design, borrow from proven systems (LSP, Mercurial, OAuth), stay boring on non-novel layers, enable self-service.

canonical_works.title: Stack Overflow Blog: No Need for Ctrl+C When You Have MCP
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Technical deep-dive on OAuth challenges for MCP, trust boundaries, supply chain security, and the risk of spec bloat.

signature_ideas: MCP is a protocol, not a platform -- keep it minimal, composable, and transport-agnostic to maximize adoption across agent runtimes.
Tool schemas should be self-documenting and typed so that LLMs can reliably invoke them without trial-and-error -- poor schemas are the primary source of tool-call failures.
Stdio transport is the right default for local agent-tool communication; streaming HTTP with session resumption is for remote. SSE is deprecated for horizontal scaling.
Sampling (letting servers request LLM completions) enables recursive agentic patterns but must be capability-gated to prevent unbounded delegation.
Resource management (list/read/subscribe) is the complement to tool-calling -- agents need both action (tools) and context (resources) for complete integration.
Over-tooling is the #1 anti-pattern: using tools for everything when resources (data/context) and prompts (user templates) are more appropriate primitives.
Shift complexity to the client side -- fewer clients than servers, so push heavy lifting there. Servers should be trivial to build.
Pain-point-first design: every MCP feature traces to a specific frustration. Don't add features speculatively.
Stay boring on non-novel layers: borrow from LSP (protocol structure), Mercurial (capability exchange), OAuth (authorization), npm (registry). Don't reinvent wheels.
Trust the LLM with raw data -- return large amounts and let the LLM sift through it. Don't over-structure or pre-format output.

quote_bank.quote: I cannot build a specialized system for everyone... I just need to enable people to build for themselves.
quote_bank.context: On MCP's purpose as an enabling protocol, not a platform

quote_bank.quote: You don't want to invent the wheel. You want to focus on the unique differences that you have.
quote_bank.context: On borrowing from proven systems (LSP, Mercurial, OAuth)

quote_bank.quote: MCP only works if it's an open ecosystem and if it's an open protocol that everyone feels safe to use, even competitors with each other.
quote_bank.context: On the Linux Foundation donation and open governance

quote_bank.quote: The heavy lift in all the MCP land is very deliberately actually on the client side, because we expect that there's way fewer clients than servers.
quote_bank.context: On the asymmetry principle for complexity placement

quote_bank.quote: When you innovate on the levels of primitives and other things, you want to stay as boring as possible for everything else.
quote_bank.context: On choosing JSON-RPC, OAuth, and other standard building blocks

quote_bank.quote: It's a very boring specification. But then, what it enables is hopefully something that looks like the current API ecosystem, but for LLM interactions.
quote_bank.context: On MCP's simplicity as a feature

quote_bank.quote: The vast majority of clients currently only do tools and tools is 10% of the spec.
We may need to let go of the traditional software engineering practices we have relied on for the past two or three decades, or even forty years.
quote_bank.context: On over-structuring tool output vs trusting LLMs with raw data

quote_bank.quote: Designing protocols requires great caution because once a mistake is made, it is basically irretrievable, as it could undermine backward compatibility.
quote_bank.context: On adding features to the core spec prematurely

quote_bank.quote: The standard is actually quite big at this point, and I think there's a risk that it can become too big.
quote_bank.context: On spec bloat and the need for extension mechanisms

core_questions: Does the MCP tool schema accurately describe the function signature, return type, and error modes?
Is the server using the right transport for the deployment context (stdio vs streaming HTTP)?
Are tool descriptions written for LLM consumption -- short, unambiguous, with examples?
Does the protocol surface support the full lifecycle: discovery, invocation, error recovery, resource cleanup?
Is the implementation using the right primitives (tools vs resources vs prompts)?
Is the server small and focused, or a monolith handling multiple unrelated services?
Does it return raw data and trust the LLM, or over-structure output?
Are trust boundaries properly isolated to prevent data exfiltration across servers?
preferred_abstractions: Small, discrete servers combined at application layer
evidence_preferences: Primitive usage distribution (tools vs resources vs prompts)

anti_patterns: Untyped or loosely typed tool parameters -- LLMs will hallucinate values
Overloading one tool with multiple concerns -- violate single responsibility
Ignoring MCP capability negotiation -- assuming all clients support all features
Embedding transport details in tool logic -- transport is separate from protocol
Missing error type annotations -- clients can't distinguish recoverable from fatal errors
Over-tooling: using tools for everything when resources or prompts are more appropriate
Over-structuring tool output: don't pre-format data, trust the LLM with raw content
Monolithic server design: should be small, discrete, combined at application layer
Adding features to core spec prematurely: use extensions for domain-specific needs
Ignoring supply chain security for untrusted MCP servers
Data exfiltration through multi-server configurations: isolate sensitive-data servers
best_used_for: Primitive usage balance review (tools vs resources vs prompts)
Trust boundary and security architecture review

playbook.title: Check primitive usage balance
playbook.context: Is the server using tools for everything, or does it properly distinguish tools (model-controlled), resources (data/context), and prompts (user templates)?
playbook.action: Audit each exposed tool and ask: is this model-controlled action (tool), application-controlled data (resource), or user-initiated template (prompt)?

playbook.title: Review server granularity
playbook.context: Is the server a monolith or properly decomposed?
playbook.action: Check if the server handles multiple unrelated service domains. Split into independent servers combined at the application layer.

playbook.title: Validate data return philosophy
playbook.context: Does the server return raw, rich data or over-structure output?
playbook.action: Review tool return values. Prefer returning large amounts of data and trusting the LLM to sift through it. Avoid pre-formatting or over-structuring.

playbook.title: Verify complexity placement
playbook.context: Is complexity on the client side where possible?
playbook.action: Check if the server does heavy processing that belongs on the client. Push complexity to the fewer-clients side.

playbook.title: Assess trust boundary isolation
playbook.context: Can the model exfiltrate data across servers?
playbook.action: For sensitive data, ensure that server providing it is isolated from other servers. Models can reshape data and insert it into other tool calls.

playbook.title: Check tool description clarity
playbook.context: Do tool descriptions overlap or confuse when multiple servers are connected?
playbook.action: Review descriptions for ambiguity. Consider if a smaller LLM could pre-filter relevant tools for the context.

playbook.title: Review supply chain security
playbook.context: Are untrusted servers treated like npm packages?
playbook.action: Ensure servers are hash-verified, allowlisted, and audited. Untrusted MCP servers carry the same risks as npm packages.

playbook.title: Evaluate transport choice
playbook.context: Is the transport appropriate for the deployment model?
playbook.action: Local servers should use stdio. Remote servers should use streaming HTTP with session resumption. SSE is deprecated for horizontal scaling.

source_confidence.rationale: Multiple Tier A sources (MAINTAINERS.md, official Anthropic announcement, Linux Foundation summit, Stack Overflow Blog) plus multiple Tier B podcast interviews with extensive direct technical content (a16z, Software Engineering Daily, Latent Space). Core expertise in MCP specification, design philosophy, anti-patterns, and evolution roadmap directly substantiated.

freshness.notes: Active as Lead Maintainer at Anthropic; MCP Dev Summit keynote April 2026 confirms ongoing role. a16z and SED podcasts from 2025-2026 contain extensive design philosophy content.
